---
title: "Same-Checkpoint Controls for Disentangling Communication Benefit from Learning Dynamics in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning"
author: "Anonymous Authors"
format:
  pdf:
    template-partials:
      - title.tex
---

```{=latex}
\begin{abstract}
Emergent communication in multi-agent reinforcement learning is often evaluated with endpoint metrics such as cooperation, mutual information, or message--action correlations measured at the final checkpoint. Under independent decentralized learning, however, such metrics can conflate two distinct questions: whether message content shaped the training trajectory, and whether the frozen endpoint policy uses the online message stream as a robust, sender-consistent, and behaviorally useful code. We introduce \emph{same-checkpoint continuation controls} to probe these questions more directly: identical learned checkpoints are forked into learned, random, and silent channel modes, and their downstream outcomes are compared during continued training. As a case study, we apply this design to a four-agent extended public goods game with hidden regime switching and noisy private observations (15 seeds). Same-checkpoint controls show that learned messages retain a clear continued-learning advantage over silence and simple random channels in the mixed-motive regime, outperforming them by roughly 12--19 percentage points. A matched-stat sender-shuffle continuation control shrinks that advantage and its confidence interval overlaps zero, suggesting a mixture of genuine state-linked message value and broader channel dependence. In the cooperation-dominant regime, learned message content shows no corresponding advantage, and sender-shuffle can outperform learned communication. Complementary frozen-checkpoint interventions show that this training-time benefit does not translate into a robust endpoint protocol: sender-shuffled messages outperform natural messages, and sender-specific causal probes reveal sizable local effects that cancel in aggregate. These results show that communication benefit and learning dynamics can be more cleanly distinguished, and that continued-learning value and endpoint protocol coherence can come apart in ways that endpoint-only evaluation misses.
\end{abstract}
```


# Introduction {#sec-intro}

Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) has produced a growing body of work on emergent communication, in which agents learn to use a discrete signaling channel to coordinate behavior [@foerster2016learning; @sukhbaatar2016learning; @lazaridou2017multi; @havrylov2017emergence; @mordatch2018emergence]. For a comprehensive survey, see @lazaridou2020emergent. Beyond classic sender--receiver benchmarks, communication is now studied in broader cooperative settings, including sequential social dilemmas, intention-sharing architectures, and zero-shot convention formation [@leibo2017multi; @kim2021communication; @hu2020other; @bullard2021quasi]. A central question is whether agents develop meaningful, shared communication protocols or merely learn policies that happen to correlate with message-sending behavior [@kottur2017natural; @cao2018emergent]. Standard evaluation practice therefore focuses on *endpoint metrics*: mutual information (MI) between messages and task-relevant variables [@lowe2019pitfalls], topographic similarity [@lazaridou2017multi], cooperation rates measured at the final checkpoint, or positive-listening / social-influence style diagnostics [@eccles2019biases; @jaques2019social].

This attribution problem is especially acute under independent learning, where non-stationarity makes it difficult to separate message semantics from the broader training dynamics [@papoudakis2019dealing; @oroojlooyjadid2022review]. We study it in a four-agent extended public goods game (EPGG) with hidden regime switching, noisy private observations, and optional binary communication [@orzan2024pgg]. The EPGG is a case study for this measurement problem rather than the contribution by itself: it gives a setting with fixed teams, sender-indexed message slots, feed-forward policies, and mixed incentives in which endpoint-only evaluation is especially likely to mislead.

Closest in spirit to our framing are critique papers that show standard emergent-communication metrics can overstate semantics. Most notably, @lowe2019pitfalls demonstrated a false-positive failure mode: messages can appear informative under endpoint analyses while exerting little or no causal influence on behavior. More recently, work on Lewis games has shown that co-adaptation and overfitting can also degrade protocol quality and generalization [@rita2022generalization]. Our contribution is complementary to these critiques. We show the opposite evaluation failure mode under independent learning: weak or brittle endpoint use does not imply that messages were unimportant during training, and same-checkpoint continuation controls provide a constructive way to probe that split.

The setting sits at the intersection of several adjacent literatures. Partially aligned signaling with hidden state is the core concern in classic cheap-talk and strategic information transmission models [@crawford1982strategic; @chen2008selecting]. Communication in public goods games also connects to experimental and institutional work on cooperation under uncertainty [@ledyard1995public; @fehr2000cooperation; @kosfeld2009institution; @orzan2024pgg]. More broadly, the paper fits within the Cooperative AI agenda of understanding how decentralized agents and institutions can achieve robust cooperation under imperfect alignment and limited information [@dafoe2020open].

We intentionally begin with independent learning rather than centralized training. The goal is not to maximize coordination with the strongest available training machinery, but to study a regime where attribution is genuinely difficult: each agent learns under non-stationarity from local observations and the message channel itself. In this setting, improvements can easily be misread as endpoint semantics when they may instead reflect broader co-learning dynamics. Independent PPO is therefore a demanding but appropriate starting point for the evaluation question we ask here.

We separate two questions that endpoint evaluation often collapses together:

```{=latex}
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*,nosep]
  \item \textbf{Continued-learning value:} Does message \emph{content} change the downstream training trajectory when we hold the starting point fixed?
  \item \textbf{Endpoint protocol coherence:} Does the frozen final policy use the online message stream as a robust, sender-consistent, and behaviorally beneficial code?
\end{enumerate}
```

Our central finding is that these two questions can have opposite answers.

The EPGG is useful here because the severity of the free-rider problem changes over time without direct notification. Communication is therefore potentially valuable, but not equally so in every regime.

Our contributions are:

```{=latex}
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*,nosep]
  \item \textbf{Same-checkpoint continuation controls.} We introduce a clean experimental design for isolating the role of message content during continued learning: fork identical learned checkpoints into learned, random, or silent channel modes and compare downstream outcomes. This separates continued-learning channel-content effects from frozen-endpoint protocol behavior within a fixed architecture and task.
  \item \textbf{Continued-learning benefit vs.\ weak endpoint coherence.} Applying this design in the EPGG case study, we find a clear late-checkpoint continued-learning advantage for learned messages in the mixed-motive regime, while frozen-checkpoint interventions reveal a much weaker endpoint protocol: sender-shuffled messages can outperform natural messages.
  \item \textbf{Strategic value-of-information in mixed regimes.} In this EPGG setting, communication matters most where incentives are mixed rather than fully aligned. In the cooperation-dominant regime, learned message content shows no advantage over silence or simple random channels, and a matched-stat sender-shuffle branch can outperform learned communication.
  \item \textbf{Endpoint causal disaggregation.} Frozen sender-specific token interventions show that endpoint messages are not inert: signed aggregate effects remain near zero while mean absolute sender--receiver effects remain sizeable, indicating substantial local influence despite weak aggregate endpoint effects.
\end{enumerate}
```


# Setup {#sec-setup}

## Extended public goods game with regime switching

We study a four-agent ($N=4$) Extended Public Goods Game (EPGG) played over $T=100$ rounds per episode. Each round $t$, agent $i$ observes a noisy private signal $\fhat_{i,t} = f_t + \varepsilon_{i,t}$, where $\varepsilon_{i,t} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma = 0.5$, and chooses to cooperate ($a_i = C$) or defect ($a_i = D$). Each agent holds an endowment of $c=4$ units. A cooperator contributes $c$ to a shared pool; a defector keeps $c$ as private payoff. Contributions are multiplied by the current regime factor $f_t$ and split equally, so each agent's round payoff is:
$$
  u_i = \frac{f_t}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} c \cdot \mathbf{1}[a_j = C] \;+\; c \cdot \mathbf{1}[a_i = D]
$$ {#eq-payoff}
where the first term is agent $i$'s share of the public pool and the second is the private endowment retained when defecting (zero when cooperating). Throughout, we report effect sizes in percentage points (pp): a change from 0.40 to 0.53 is $+13\pp$.

The hidden multiplier $f_t$ takes values in $\mathcal{F} = \{0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5, 5.0\}$ and switches with probability $\rho = 0.05$ per round (geometric holding time ${\sim}20$ rounds). Each agent's action is subject to trembling: with probability $\varepsilon_{\text{tremble}} = 0.05$, the action is replaced by a uniform random choice, modeling execution noise.

When $f < N = 4$, defection is the dominant strategy; when $f > N$, cooperation dominates regardless of others' actions. The mixed-motive regime ($f = 3.5$) is the critical case: cooperation is collectively beneficial but individually costly ($f/N = 0.875 < 1$, so each unit contributed returns less than one unit to the contributor). Concretely, a sole defector among three cooperators earns $14.5$ versus $14.0$ for a cooperator at $f\!=\!3.5$ (a temptation of $+0.5$ per round), but earns $19.0$ versus $20.0$ at $f\!=\!5.0$ (a penalty of $-1.0$). This regime creates the strongest demand for communication. The full multiplier set spans five regimes, but the contrast between $f\!=\!3.5$ and $f\!=\!5.0$ is theoretically central because it separates ambiguous mixed incentives from a regime where cooperation is individually optimal. Unless otherwise noted, reported quantities such as $\pcoopgiven{f}$ condition on the \emph{true round-level multiplier} $f_t$ during evaluation rather than on an episode-level label.

## Communication channel

In the communication condition (`comm_symm`), agents simultaneously broadcast a binary message $m_i \in \{0, 1\}$ before choosing actions. All agents observe the same sender-indexed message vector. Messages have no pre-assigned meaning; any semantics must emerge through learning. In the no-communication condition (`no_comm_symm`), no messages are exchanged. Agents never observe the true multiplier $f_t$, other agents' private signals, or other agents' rewards.

## Training

We train agents using independent PPO [@schulman2017proximal] with generalized advantage estimation [@schulman2016high] ($\gamma = 0.99$, $\lambda = 0.95$, clip ratio $= 0.2$). Each agent uses separate two-hidden-layer MLPs (hidden width 64) for the action policy and value function; sender agents additionally use a separate two-hidden-layer MLP for the message policy. At each round, a sender first emits a message from the wrapped Set-A observation available before same-round message delivery, and all agents then select actions conditioned on the received same-round message vector. For sender-updated samples, the action head and message head are optimized jointly with one clipped objective over $\log \pi(a_t, m_t)$, using the agent's own reward return and the same GAE advantage; the shared value network predicts only that agent's own discounted return. The policies are not recurrent. Instead, temporal information enters through a trainer-side observation wrapper that converts the raw environment observation $[\fhat, \text{endowment}]$ into Set-A inputs $[\fhat, \text{endowment}, \text{last-round cooperation fraction}, \text{own last action}, \text{EWMA cooperation}]$, where EWMA denotes an exponential moving average, plus one two-entry one-hot block per sender when communication is enabled. The base wrapped observation is therefore 5-dimensional; with four senders and binary messages, the communication slice adds eight dimensions (two per sender), giving a 13-dimensional actor input in the communication condition. At message time this message slice reflects previously delivered messages (all zeros at episode start), and after delivery the action policy sees the rebuilt same-round message vector. The value network receives the same features plus a normalized within-episode time scalar, giving 14 dimensions by default. During training, each sender's delivered token is independently corrupted with probability 0.1 by resampling from that sender's empirical token marginal; this message-dropout corruption affects online sampled trajectories but not the frozen-checkpoint perturbation analyses. Agents therefore track the sticky latent regime only through current noisy signals, these simple lagged summaries, and communication available through the wrapped observation. Key design choices:

**Why independent PPO.** As argued in the introduction, we use independent PPO to keep the attribution problem in the decentralized regime rather than partly resolving it through centralized training signals.

**Entropy annealing.** Both action and message entropy coefficients decay linearly over training: action entropy from $0.01 \to 0.001$, message entropy from $0.01 \to 0.0$. This allows early exploration while permitting conventions to crystallize. The learning rate follows a cosine schedule from $3 \times 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$.

**Seeds.** Fifteen independent seeds per condition, trained for 150k episodes with checkpoints at 50k, 100k, and 150k.

## Evaluation suite {#sec-eval}

All evaluation uses greedy action selection (argmax policy) to isolate learned behavior from exploration noise. These greedy checkpoint evaluations are our main evidence about policy quality over training. By contrast, the dense online training curves introduced later are descriptive context only: they summarize sampled on-policy behavior during training and therefore still reflect tremble noise, message dropout, and residual exploration. Unless otherwise noted, means, SEMs, bootstrap confidence intervals, and sign-flip tests treat training seed as the unit of analysis; paired tests operate on per-seed paired deltas. We evaluate four diagnostic dimensions:

**Message interventions.** At each checkpoint, we freeze the policy and replay episodes while tampering with the messages agents receive. The interventions test different hypotheses about what matters in the message stream:

```{=latex}
\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*,nosep]
  \item \texttt{zeros}: zero the entire message slice of the wrapped observation after message construction---tests whether the policy benefits from \emph{any} message input.
  \item \texttt{fixed0}: replace every sender slot with token~0 before one-hot encoding---tests a constant but non-empty message pattern.
  \item \texttt{indep\_random}: replace sender slots with independent random bits each round---tests whether slot-wise noise is enough.
  \item \texttt{public\_random}: replace all sender slots with the same random bit each round---tests whether a shared coordination cue suffices.
  \item \texttt{sender\_shuffle}: for each sender, replace the current token with one drawn from that sender's empirical token pool---preserving per-sender marginal token frequency while breaking alignment with current state and time.
  \item \texttt{permute\_slots}: randomly permute sender slots each round while preserving the delivered bits---tests whether receivers track \emph{who} sent a message.
\end{itemize}
```

These frozen-checkpoint perturbations estimate the effect of specific endpoint message manipulations; they do not, by themselves, identify the full causal value of communication over training.

**Channel controls.** We compare four channel modes during *continued training* from the same learned 50k checkpoint: \texttt{learned} (sender's actual message), \texttt{always\_zero} (all sender slots forced to token~0), \texttt{indep\_random} (sender slots randomized independently each round), and \texttt{public\_random} (all sender slots share the same random bit each round). This tests whether message \emph{content} matters for continued learning once initialization is held fixed.

**Sender and receiver semantics.** Appendix~C summarizes sender message rates and receiver cooperation by discretizing each agent's noisy private signal into five bins and conditioning on sender identity. The full bin definitions and notation appear there.

**Sender-specific causal endpoint probe.** At the frozen 150k endpoint, we take greedy-evaluation states from the natural rollout, hold the raw observations and all other message slots fixed, force sender $i$'s token to 0 or 1, and measure the induced change in receiver $j$'s cooperation probability and greedy action. Unlike the receiver-semantics summaries, this is an interventional test of whether sender-conditioned endpoint effects are causally used by the frozen policy.

These diagnostics answer different questions. Same-checkpoint continuation isolates whether channel content affects \emph{continued learning} when initialization is fixed. Frozen message interventions ask whether the \emph{endpoint policy} benefits from the natural online message stream. Sender-specific frozen interventions ask whether individual sender tokens retain local causal influence at the endpoint. Receiver-semantics analyses are descriptive rather than causal and are included to show how aggregate endpoint summaries can hide sender-conditioned structure.

**Roadmap.** The remaining sections are organized as follows. The [baseline comparison](#sec-main-result) establishes a descriptive baseline by comparing separately trained communication and no-communication policies. The [continued-learning analysis](#sec-semantic-window) presents the paper's core identification design: [same-checkpoint continuation](#sec-sameckpt) and [frozen-checkpoint endpoint interventions](#sec-frozen-endpoint). The [Discussion](#sec-discussion) then places the results in context and discusses limitations, while Appendices~C--D provide complementary endpoint and mechanism diagnostics.

# Baseline communication gap in separately trained policies {#sec-main-result}

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Round-conditional cooperation rates $P(C \mid f_t=f)$ for communication vs.\ no-communication conditions across training. Mean $\pm$ SEM over 15 seeds. The final row reports the paired mean gap (\texttt{comm\_symm} minus \texttt{no\_comm\_symm}).}
  \tablabel{comm_gap}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{llccc}
    \toprule
    & & \multicolumn{3}{c}{Checkpoint (episodes)} \\
    \cmidrule(lr){3-5}
    $f$ regime & Condition & 50k & 100k & 150k \\
    \midrule
    \multirow{3}{*}{$f\!=\!3.5$}
      & \texttt{comm\_symm}     & $\TIcommTFFk$ & $\TIcommTFHk$ & $\TIcommTFOfk$ \\
      & \texttt{no\_comm\_symm} & $\TIncTFFk$ & $\TIncTFHk$ & $\TIncTFOfk$ \\
      & $\commgap$              & $\TIgapTFFk$ & $\TIgapTFHk$ & $\TIgapTFOfk$ \\
    \midrule
    \multirow{3}{*}{$f\!=\!5.0$}
      & \texttt{comm\_symm}     & $\TIcommFVFk$ & $\TIcommFVHk$ & $\TIcommFVOfk$ \\
      & \texttt{no\_comm\_symm} & $\TIncFVFk$ & $\TIncFVHk$ & $\TIncFVOfk$ \\
      & $\commgap$              & $\TIgapFVFk$ & $\TIgapFVHk$ & $\TIgapFVOfk$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

We begin with a separately trained baseline comparison. Because the communication and no-communication policies are trained independently, the gap in Table \ref{tab:comm_gap} mixes message effects with broader training-trajectory differences; we use it to motivate the tighter same-checkpoint analysis below. @fig-value-surface later shows that this descriptive gap peaks in the upper mixed regimes rather than increasing monotonically with generic ``difficulty.''

At $f = 3.5$ (mixed regime), communication produces a robust $+\pvGapTFFkPP\pp$ cooperation advantage at 50k episodes, with all 15 seeds showing positive effects (range: $+\pvGapRangeLo$ to $+\pvGapRangeHi\pp$). This benefit persists more modestly at 150k ($+\pvGapTFOfkPP\pp$, $\pvNposOfk$/15 seeds positive).

At $f = 5.0$ (cooperation-dominant regime), the communication advantage is small at 50k ($+\pvGapFVFkPP\pp$) and reverses by 150k ($\pvGapFVOfkPP\pp$, $p < 0.001$). Appendix~D weighs more strongly against a pure architecture-slowing account and toward late mixed-regime conditioning: communication is not behind early at $f\!=\!5.0$, history interventions matter more than message corruption at 150k, and same-checkpoint controls show no learned-content advantage there.

Communication helps most where latent-state information matters under mixed incentives, and the $f\!=\!3.5$ benefit declines from 50k to 150k without disappearing entirely.


# When does message content matter? {#sec-semantic-window}

The communication benefit raises a question: does message *content* drive the cooperation advantage, or does merely having a channel (even a noisy one) suffice? We address this with a sequence of controls centered on same-checkpoint continuation.

## Same-checkpoint continuation isolates continued-training channel-content effects {#sec-sameckpt}

The separately trained comparison in Table \ref{tab:comm_gap} confounds message effects with everything else that differs between training runs. To remove that confound, we hold the starting point fixed: all branches begin from the **same** learned 50k checkpoints. We then fork those checkpoints into four basic channel controls plus one matched-stat continuation control ($N = 15$ seeds).

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Same-checkpoint continuation controls: cooperation when all modes start from identical learned 50k policies. At 50k, all values are identical (same checkpoint). By 150k, the mixed-motive branch ($f\!=\!3.5$) retains a clear learned-channel advantage, whereas the cooperation-dominant regime ($f\!=\!5.0$) shows little separation. The matched-stat \texttt{sender\_shuffle} row preserves per-sender token frequencies while breaking state-contingency. Mean $\pm$ SEM over \TIIInSeeds{} seeds; $\Delta$ column shows the paired learned$-$control gap at 150k with 95\% bootstrap CI.}
  \tablabel{sameckpt}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{lccccc}
    \toprule
    & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$f\!=\!3.5$} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$f\!=\!5.0$} & \\
    \cmidrule(lr){2-3} \cmidrule(lr){4-5}
    Channel mode & 50k & 150k & 50k & 150k & $\Delta$ at 150k ($f\!=\!3.5$) \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{learned}        & $\TIIILearnedFk$ & $\mathbf{\TIIILearnedOfk}$ & $\TIIILearnedFkFV$ & $\TIIILearnedOfkFV$ & --- \\
    \texttt{indep\_random}  & $\TIIIIndepFk$ & $\TIIIIndepOfk$ & $\TIIIIndepFkFV$ & $\TIIIIndepOfkFV$ & $\TIIIIndepGapOfk\pp\;\TIIIIndepGapOfkCI$ \\
    \texttt{public\_random} & $\TIIIPubFk$ & $\TIIIPubOfk$ & $\TIIIPubFkFV$ & $\TIIIPubOfkFV$ & $\TIIIPubGapOfk\pp\;\TIIIPubGapOfkCI$ \\
    \texttt{always\_zero}   & $\TIIIZeroFk$ & $\TIIIZeroOfk$ & $\TIIIZeroFkFV$ & $\TIIIZeroOfkFV$ & $\TIIIZeroGapOfk\pp\;\TIIIZeroGapOfkCI$ \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{sender\_shuffle} & $\TIIILearnedFk$ & $\TSSshufCoopTFOfkPM$ & $\TIIILearnedFkFV$ & $\TSSshufCoopFVOfkPM$ & $\TSSgapTFOfk\pp\;\TSSgapTFOfkCI$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

Table \ref{tab:sameckpt} therefore speaks to continued-training effects rather than a full-run decomposition. By 150k at $f\!=\!3.5$, learned messages retain paired advantages of $\TIIIZeroGapOfk\pp$ versus silence (95\% CI $\TIIIZeroGapOfkCI$, sign-flip $p\TIIIZeroGapOfkP$), $\TIIIPubGapOfk\pp$ versus public random (CI $\TIIIPubGapOfkCI$, $p\TIIIPubGapOfkP$), and $\TIIIIndepGapOfk\pp$ versus independent random (CI $\TIIIIndepGapOfkCI$, $p\TIIIIndepGapOfkP$). All three paired confidence intervals exclude zero, but all branches decline from the shared 50k starting point: the learned branch does not \emph{sustain} cooperation, it \emph{slows} the decline relative to the controls. A natural concern is channel-distribution shift: the learned-minus-control gap may partly reflect adaptation cost from receiving unfamiliar inputs rather than semantic value. The matched-stat \texttt{sender\_shuffle} continuation addresses this by replacing each sender's token with a draw from that sender's empirical marginal, preserving on-distribution marginals while destroying state-contingency. If learned still outperforms sender-shuffle, the advantage is harder to attribute to distribution matching alone. At 150k and $f\!=\!3.5$, learned still exceeds sender-shuffle on average (53.6\% vs.\ 44.6\% cooperation; $+9.0\pp$ mean paired gap), but this gap is heterogeneous and its bootstrap CI overlaps zero; at $f\!=\!5.0$, sender-shuffle instead outperforms learned by $15.9\pp$. The cleanest interpretation is therefore intermediate: the learned-vs-zero/random advantage combines genuine state-linked message value with broader channel dependence, and the semantic component is real but more modest under matched-stat controls.

@fig-value-surface makes the multiplier-wide version of this point visible before we turn to the endpoint intervention suite.

```{python}
#| label: fig-value-surface
#| fig-cap: "Full multiplier surface for three key 150k contrasts. Left: separately trained communication minus no-communication endpoint gap. Middle: same-checkpoint learned minus silence continuation gap. Right: frozen natural minus sender-shuffle endpoint gap. The continued-learning content effect peaks in the upper mixed regimes ($f\\!=\\!2.5, 3.5$), while the raw endpoint gap reverses at $f\\!=\\!5.0$ and sender-shuffling is most harmful to the natural endpoint protocol at $f\\!=\\!2.5, 3.5$."
#| fig-pos: "t"
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import csv
from pathlib import Path

DATA_ROOT = Path("../../outputs/eval")
VALUE_SURFACE = DATA_ROOT / "paper_strengthen/iter0_value_surface/value_surface_summary.csv"

with open(VALUE_SURFACE) as f:
    rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))

series = [
    ("main_gap", "learned_vs_baseline", "Comm - no comm", "#238b45"),
    ("sameckpt_gap", "learned_vs_fixed0", "Same ckpt: learned - silence", "#2171b5"),
    ("frozen_gap", "learned_vs_sender_shuffle", "Frozen: natural - shuffle", "#cb181d"),
]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(7.2, 2.6), sharey=True)

for ax, (family, contrast, title, color) in zip(axes, series):
    subset = [
        r for r in rows
        if r["family"] == family and r["contrast"] == contrast and r["metric"] == "coop_rate"
    ]
    subset = sorted(subset, key=lambda r: float(r["f_value"]))
    xs = [float(r["f_value"]) for r in subset]
    ys = [100.0 * float(r["delta_mean"]) for r in subset]
    lo = [100.0 * float(r["delta_ci_low"]) for r in subset]
    hi = [100.0 * float(r["delta_ci_high"]) for r in subset]
    yerr = [
        [y - l for y, l in zip(ys, lo)],
        [h - y for y, h in zip(ys, hi)],
    ]

    ax.errorbar(xs, ys, yerr=yerr, fmt="o-", color=color, lw=1.6, ms=4.5, capsize=3)
    ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.6)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=8.5)
    ax.set_xticks(xs)
    ax.set_xlabel("$f$")
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)

axes[0].set_ylabel("150k contrast (pp)")
axes[0].set_ylim(-30, 30)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-semantic-window
#| fig-cap: "Same-checkpoint continuation controls at 150k episodes (15 seeds). \\textbf{Left:} Paired learned$-$control deltas at $f\\!=\\!3.5$ for each seed. \\textbf{Right:} Raw per-seed cooperation at $f\\!=\\!5.0$ across the four channel modes. Black squares show mean $\\pm$ SEM."
#| fig-pos: "t"
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import csv
import statistics
from pathlib import Path

DATA_ROOT = Path("../../outputs/eval")
CTRL_SAMECKPT_RAW = DATA_ROOT / "phase3_sameckpt_continuation_15seeds_local_20260319/report/channel_control_raw.csv"

with open(CTRL_SAMECKPT_RAW) as f:
    rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))

def mean(vals):
    return sum(vals) / len(vals) if vals else float("nan")

def sem(vals):
    return statistics.stdev(vals) / len(vals) ** 0.5 if len(vals) > 1 else 0.0

def offsets(n, width=0.18):
    if n <= 1:
        return [0.0]
    center = (n - 1) / 2.0
    return [width * (i - center) / center for i in range(n)]

control_modes = ["always_zero", "public_random", "indep_random"]
control_labels = {
    "always_zero": "Always\nzero",
    "public_random": "Public\nrandom",
    "indep_random": "Indep.\nrandom",
}
control_colors = {
    "always_zero": "#969696",
    "public_random": "#bdd7e7",
    "indep_random": "#6baed6",
}

mode_order = ["learned", "indep_random", "public_random", "always_zero"]
mode_labels = {
    "learned": "Learned",
    "indep_random": "Indep.\nrandom",
    "public_random": "Public\nrandom",
    "always_zero": "Always\nzero",
}
mode_colors = {
    "learned": "#2171b5",
    "indep_random": "#6baed6",
    "public_random": "#bdd7e7",
    "always_zero": "#969696",
}

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(6.2, 2.9))

# Left panel: paired deltas learned - control at f=3.5, 150k
per_mode = {m: {} for m in ["learned"] + control_modes}
for r in rows:
    if r.get("f_value") != "3.500" or int(float(r["checkpoint_episode"])) != 150_000:
        continue
    mode = r["mode"]
    if mode in per_mode:
        per_mode[mode][int(float(r["train_seed"]))] = float(r["coop_rate"])

for x, mode in enumerate(control_modes):
    seeds = sorted(set(per_mode["learned"]) & set(per_mode[mode]))
    vals = [per_mode["learned"][s] - per_mode[mode][s] for s in seeds]
    xs = [x + d for d in offsets(len(vals))]
    ax1.scatter(xs, vals, s=18, color=control_colors[mode], edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.9)
    ax1.errorbar(
        x,
        mean(vals),
        yerr=sem(vals),
        fmt="s",
        color="black",
        mfc="white",
        mec="black",
        ms=5,
        capsize=3,
        lw=0.9,
        zorder=5,
    )

ax1.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.6)
ax1.set_xticks(range(len(control_modes)))
ax1.set_xticklabels([control_labels[m] for m in control_modes], fontsize=7)
ax1.set_ylabel("Learned - control $\\Delta P(C)$ at 150k")
ax1.set_title("$f\\!=\\!3.5$ paired deltas", fontsize=9)

# Right panel: raw per-seed cooperation at f=5.0, 150k
per_mode_50 = {m: {} for m in mode_order}
for r in rows:
    if r.get("f_value") != "5.000" or int(float(r["checkpoint_episode"])) != 150_000:
        continue
    mode = r["mode"]
    if mode in per_mode_50:
        per_mode_50[mode][int(float(r["train_seed"]))] = float(r["coop_rate"])

for x, mode in enumerate(mode_order):
    seeds = sorted(per_mode_50[mode])
    vals = [per_mode_50[mode][s] for s in seeds]
    xs = [x + d for d in offsets(len(vals))]
    ax2.scatter(xs, vals, s=18, color=mode_colors[mode], edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.9)
    ax2.errorbar(
        x,
        mean(vals),
        yerr=sem(vals),
        fmt="s",
        color="black",
        mfc="white",
        mec="black",
        ms=5,
        capsize=3,
        lw=0.9,
        zorder=5,
    )

ax2.set_xticks(range(len(mode_order)))
ax2.set_xticklabels([mode_labels[m] for m in mode_order], fontsize=7)
ax2.set_ylabel("$P(C)$ at 150k")
ax2.set_title("$f\\!=\\!5.0$ raw outcomes", fontsize=9)
ax2.set_ylim(0, 1.0)

for ax in (ax1, ax2):
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Frozen-checkpoint endpoint use is mixed and non-robust at 150k {#sec-frozen-endpoint}

The preceding experiment shows that message content matters for \emph{learning}. We now ask a different question: does the \emph{already-trained} policy still benefit from natural messages during evaluation, with no further learning? The answer is much weaker.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption[Frozen-checkpoint intervention effects at $f\!=\!3.5$ and 150k.]{Frozen-checkpoint intervention effects in the mixed-motive regime ($f\!=\!3.5$) at the 150k checkpoint. $\Delta P(C)$ in percentage points, computed as natural $-$ intervened: positive values mean natural messages helped, negative values mean the intervention \emph{outperformed} natural messages. Mean $\pm$ SEM over 15 seeds; 95\% bootstrap CI and sign consistency (fraction of seeds where natural $>$ intervention) are reported for 150k only. The corresponding 50k endpoint summaries appear in Appendix~C.}
  \tablabel{frozen_endpoint}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{lccl}
    \toprule
    Intervention & 150k & 95\% CI & Sign \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{zeros}          & $\TFRZerosOfk \pm \TFRZerosOfkSEM$ & $\TFRZerosOfkCI$ & $\TFRZerosOfkSign$ \\
    \texttt{fixed0}         & $\TFRFixZeroOfk \pm \TFRFixZeroOfkSEM$ & $\TFRFixZeroOfkCI$ & $\TFRFixZeroOfkSign$ \\
    \texttt{public\_random} & $\TFRPubOfk \pm \TFRPubOfkSEM$ & $\TFRPubOfkCI$ & $\TFRPubOfkSign$ \\
    \texttt{indep\_random}  & $\TFRIndepOfk \pm \TFRIndepOfkSEM$ & $\TFRIndepOfkCI$ & $\TFRIndepOfkSign$ \\
    \texttt{sender\_shuffle} & $\TFRShuffleOfk \pm \TFRShuffleOfkSEM$ & $\TFRShuffleOfkCI$ & $\TFRShuffleOfkSign$ \\
    \texttt{permute\_slots} & $\TFRPermuteOfk \pm \TFRPermuteOfkSEM$ & $\TFRPermuteOfkCI$ & $\TFRPermuteOfkSign$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

Table \ref{tab:frozen_endpoint} shows that 150k endpoint intervention effects are modest relative to the same-checkpoint continuation gaps. The corresponding 50k endpoint summaries are reported in Appendix~C and are also small in aggregate. The main exception is \texttt{sender\_shuffle}, which \emph{outperforms} natural messages at 150k ($\TFRShuffleOfk\pp$; 95\% CI $\TFRShuffleOfkCI\pp$; sign-flip $p\TFRShuffleOfkP$). Because sender-shuffling preserves each sender's marginal token frequency while breaking state-contingency, this result implies that the frozen endpoint policy does not robustly benefit from the original state-conditioned message stream. But are individual messages still doing \emph{anything}? To test this, we next intervene on one sender token at a time while holding the rest of the message vector fixed, in the spirit of intervention-based influence analyses [@jaques2019social].

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Frozen sender-specific causal probe at 150k, non-self sender--receiver pairs only. For each true multiplier $f$, we hold the raw observation and other message slots fixed, force sender $i$'s token to 0 or 1, and compute the resulting signed and absolute change in receiver cooperation probability. Flip-rate columns report the fraction of states in which forcing token 0 or 1 changes receiver $j$'s greedy action relative to the natural message. Effect summaries include 95\% bootstrap CIs over training seeds.}
  \tablabel{sender_causal}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{lcccc}
    \toprule
    $f$ & Signed $\Delta P(C)$ (pp) & Mean $|\Delta P(C)|$ (pp) & Flip rate force0 (\%) & Flip rate force1 (\%) \\
    \midrule
    $0.5$ & $\TSCDeltaZeroFive\;\TSCDeltaZeroFiveCI$ & $\TSCAbsZeroFive\;\TSCAbsZeroFiveCI$ & $\TSCFlipZeroZeroFive$ & $\TSCFlipOneZeroFive$ \\
    $1.5$ & $\TSCDeltaOneFive\;\TSCDeltaOneFiveCI$ & $\TSCAbsOneFive\;\TSCAbsOneFiveCI$ & $\TSCFlipZeroOneFive$ & $\TSCFlipOneOneFive$ \\
    $2.5$ & $\TSCDeltaTwoFive\;\TSCDeltaTwoFiveCI$ & $\TSCAbsTwoFive\;\TSCAbsTwoFiveCI$ & $\TSCFlipZeroTwoFive$ & $\TSCFlipOneTwoFive$ \\
    $3.5$ & $\TSCDeltaThreeFive\;\TSCDeltaThreeFiveCI$ & $\TSCAbsThreeFive\;\TSCAbsThreeFiveCI$ & $\TSCFlipZeroThreeFive$ & $\TSCFlipOneThreeFive$ \\
    $5.0$ & $\TSCDeltaFiveZero\;\TSCDeltaFiveZeroCI$ & $\TSCAbsFiveZero\;\TSCAbsFiveZeroCI$ & $\TSCFlipZeroFiveZero$ & $\TSCFlipOneFiveZero$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

Table \ref{tab:sender_causal} answers the local endpoint question directly: individual messages still exert local causal effects. For non-self sender--receiver pairs, forcing a single sender's token flips the receiver's greedy action $\TSCFlipZeroThreeFive$--$\TSCFlipOneThreeFive$\% of the time at $f\!=\!3.5$, with mean absolute cooperation effects of $\TSCAbsThreeFive\pp$; at $f\!=\!5.0$, the corresponding values are $\TSCFlipZeroFiveZero$--$\TSCFlipOneFiveZero$\% and $\TSCAbsFiveZero\pp$. Yet the signed mean effect is near zero at every multiplier, including $\TSCDeltaThreeFive\pp$ at $f\!=\!3.5$ and $\TSCDeltaFiveZero\pp$ at $f\!=\!5.0$. The endpoint channel therefore has real local causal effects that largely cancel in aggregate. This pattern is consistent with different senders developing opposite conventions: one sender's token~1 encourages cooperation while another's discourages it, so the per-sender effects wash out when averaged together.

Non-self effects are nearly as large as self effects (Appendix~C), so the endpoint channel is not merely a self-conditioning device.

## Synthesis: separating continued-learning from endpoint effects

Same-checkpoint continuation and frozen-endpoint interventions answer different questions. In this EPGG case study:

```{=latex}
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*,nosep]
  \item \textbf{Continued-learning value survives in the mixed-motive regime, but weakens under matched-stat controls.} At $f\!=\!3.5$, learned messages outperform all control modes at 150k (Table \ref{tab:sameckpt}). A matched-stat sender-shuffle branch narrows that gap substantially and leaves its CI overlapping zero, indicating that the strongest learned-vs-control separation reflects a mixture of state-linked message value and broader channel dependence rather than a purely semantic channel.
  \item \textbf{Endpoint online use is much weaker and can be adverse.} The frozen-endpoint interventions are small relative to the same-checkpoint gaps, and \texttt{sender\_shuffle} improves performance. Yet the sender-specific causal probe still finds $\TSCAbsThreeFive\pp$ mean absolute non-self effects at $f\!=\!3.5$ despite a signed average near zero. Endpoint communication is therefore locally causal but not organized into a robust shared protocol. This is \emph{aggregate cancellation}: sender-indexed conventions with opposing polarities produce near-zero aggregate effects despite strong per-sender responses.
  \item \textbf{The $f\!=\!5.0$ reversal reflects late history-conditioned miscalibration.} The communication branch drifts downward from 50k to 150k in both regimes, but only $f\!=\!5.0$ turns this drift into a reversal: at $f\!=\!3.5$, learned messages still beat the control modes, whereas at $f\!=\!5.0$ same-checkpoint controls show no learned-content advantage and matched-stat sender-shuffling outperforms learned communication. Appendix~D further shows that the late $f\!=\!5.0$ shortfall is more sensitive to temporal-history interventions than to message corruption, consistent with a failure to disengage mixed-regime conditionality. This is a \emph{strategic value-of-information} failure: evaluating only at $f\!=\!5.0$ would miss the genuine content effect at $f\!=\!3.5$.
\end{enumerate}
```

Aggregate endpoint summaries can still miss sender-conditioned structure because opposing per-sender effects cancel in the mean. Appendix~C reports that observational disaggregation and polarity analysis; the main identification rests on the paired continuation controls and frozen causal probes above.


# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

The preceding results establish a dissociation between continued-learning value and endpoint protocol coherence. In this EPGG case study, messages still change learning in the mixed regime even when the final endpoint code is brittle and sender-indexed, underscoring that endpoint summaries alone can misstate communication benefit under independent learning [@papoudakis2019dealing; @oroojlooyjadid2022review].

The results remain conditional on one game, a binary vocabulary, four fixed-team agents with sender-labeled slots, and independent PPO. The same-checkpoint design holds initialization fixed but does not fully remove channel-distribution shift, and our matched-stat sender-shuffle control still preserves only per-sender marginals rather than richer temporal structure or a clean self-versus-other decomposition. The continuation branches are warm starts rather than bitwise uninterrupted runs, and all frozen evaluations use a fixed evaluation seed (9001), so uncertainty is over training seeds rather than evaluator-seed sweeps. The most immediate next step is a stricter matched-history continuation design that also separates self-versus-other dependence and uses earlier or multiple branch points.


## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

\appendix

```{=latex}
\clearpage
```

# Appendix A: Full experimental parameters {#sec-app-params}

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Complete hyperparameter specification for all training runs.}
  \tablabel{hyperparams}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{ll}
    \toprule
    Parameter & Value \\
    \midrule
    \multicolumn{2}{l}{\textit{Environment}} \\
    Number of agents $N$ & 4 \\
    Episode length $T$ & 100 rounds \\
    Multiplier set $\mathcal{F}$ & $\{0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5, 5.0\}$ \\
    Regime switch probability $\rho$ & 0.05 \\
    Signal noise $\sigma$ & 0.5 (per agent) \\
    Tremble probability $\varepsilon_{\text{tremble}}$ & 0.05 \\
    Endowment $c$ & 4 \\
    \midrule
    \multicolumn{2}{l}{\textit{Architecture}} \\
    Policy/value networks & Each agent: separate 2-layer MLPs for action and value; sender agents additionally have a separate 2-layer message MLP \\
    Hidden size / activation & 64 / ReLU \\
    Recurrent state & None \\
    Observation wrapper & $[\fhat,\ \text{endowment},\ \text{last coop. frac.},\ \text{own last action},\ \text{EWMA coop.},\ \text{received one-hot messages}]$ \\
    Actor input dim. (\texttt{comm\_symm}) & 13 (5 wrapped base features + 8 message features) \\
    Value input dim. (\texttt{comm\_symm}) & 14 (actor input + normalized within-episode time) \\
    Vocabulary size $|M|$ & 2 (binary) \\
    Parameters per network & ${\sim}5$K scale \\
    \midrule
    \multicolumn{2}{l}{\textit{PPO}} \\
    Discount $\gamma$ & 0.99 \\
    GAE $\lambda$ & 0.95 \\
    Clip ratio & 0.2 \\
    Reward scale & 20.0 \\
    \midrule
    \multicolumn{2}{l}{\textit{Schedules}} \\
    Learning rate & $3 \times 10^{-4} \to 10^{-5}$ (cosine) \\
    Action entropy coeff. & $0.01 \to 0.001$ (linear) \\
    Message entropy coeff. & $0.01 \to 0.0$ (linear) \\
    Optimizer & Adam, one optimizer per agent over all trainable parameters \\
    \midrule
    \multicolumn{2}{l}{\textit{Training}} \\
    Total episodes & 150,000 \\
    Seeds & 15 independent seeds \\
    Checkpoint interval & 50,000 episodes \\
    Message dropout & 0.1 per sender per round; dropped token resampled from sender empirical marginal \\
    \midrule
    \multicolumn{2}{l}{\textit{Evaluation}} \\
    Action selection & Greedy (argmax) \\
    Evaluation episodes & 300 per (checkpoint, seed, condition) \\
    Evaluation seed & 9001 (fixed) \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

# Appendix B: Per-seed breakdown of main results {#sec-app-per-seed}

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Summary statistics for cooperation rates and communication gap at $f\!=\!3.5$ across training ($n = \pvPSnSeedsFk$ seeds).}
  \tablabel{per_seed_summary}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{llccc}
    \toprule
    & & \multicolumn{3}{c}{Checkpoint (episodes)} \\
    \cmidrule(lr){3-5}
    Metric & Statistic & 50k & 100k & 150k \\
    \midrule
    \multirow{3}{*}{Comm $P(C)$}
      & Mean & $\pvPScommMeanFk$ & $\pvPScommMeanHk$ & $\pvPScommMeanOfk$ \\
      & Min & $\pvPScommMinFk$ & $\pvPScommMinHk$ & $\pvPScommMinOfk$ \\
      & Max & $\pvPScommMaxFk$ & $\pvPScommMaxHk$ & $\pvPScommMaxOfk$ \\
    \midrule
    \multirow{3}{*}{Comm gap $\Delta$}
      & Mean & $\pvPSgapMeanFk$ & $\pvPSgapMeanHk$ & $\pvPSgapMeanOfk$ \\
      & Min & $\pvPSgapMinFk$ & $\pvPSgapMinHk$ & $\pvPSgapMinOfk$ \\
      & Max & $\pvPSgapMaxFk$ & $\pvPSgapMaxHk$ & $\pvPSgapMaxOfk$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

# Appendix C: Aggregate endpoint metrics and polarity alignment {#sec-app-alignment}

The frozen sender-specific interventions in the main text show that endpoint messages can still have heterogeneous local causal effects. This appendix adds the descriptive complement: aggregate receiver-semantics summaries can nevertheless look near zero once they average across senders.

**Important caveat.** The receiver-semantics effects reported here are *observational* (conditioned on sender identity), not interventional. They describe sender-conditioned structure but do not isolate a causal mechanism.

For the sender- and receiver-semantics summaries in this appendix, we discretize each agent's noisy private signal into five bins, denoted $\fhat_{\text{bin}}$: $\fhat < 1.5$ (low), $1.5 \le \fhat < 2.5$, $2.5 \le \fhat < 3.5$, $3.5 \le \fhat < 4.5$, and $\fhat \ge 4.5$ (high). We then summarize $P(m_i = 1 \mid \fhat_{\text{bin}})$ and $P(a_j = C \mid m_i, \fhat_{\text{bin}})$ by sender identity. When we refer to low and high bins for polarity, we mean $\fhat < 1.5$ and $\fhat \ge 4.5$, respectively.

## Early frozen-endpoint summaries

For completeness, Table \ref{tab:frozen_endpoint_50k} reports the frozen endpoint intervention effects at 50k for the same mixed-motive regime shown in the main text. These early endpoint effects are already small in aggregate, which is why the main text focuses Table \ref{tab:frozen_endpoint} on the final 150k checkpoint.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Frozen-checkpoint intervention effects in the mixed-motive regime ($f\!=\!3.5$) at the 50k checkpoint. $\Delta P(C)$ is computed as natural $-$ intervened, so positive values mean natural messages helped and negative values mean the intervention outperformed natural messages. Mean $\pm$ SEM over 15 seeds.}
  \tablabel{frozen_endpoint_50k}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{lc}
    \toprule
    Intervention & 50k \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{zeros}           & $\TFRZerosFk \pm \TFRZerosFkSEM$ \\
    \texttt{fixed0}          & $\TFRFixZeroFk \pm \TFRFixZeroFkSEM$ \\
    \texttt{public\_random}  & $\TFRPubFk \pm \TFRPubFkSEM$ \\
    \texttt{indep\_random}   & $\TFRIndepFk \pm \TFRIndepFkSEM$ \\
    \texttt{sender\_shuffle} & $\TFRShuffleFk \pm \TFRShuffleFkSEM$ \\
    \texttt{permute\_slots}  & $\TFRPermuteFk \pm \TFRPermuteFkSEM$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

For completeness on self-conditioning, the 150k frozen sender-causal probe also shows that self-pair effects are only modestly larger than non-self effects: mean absolute self-pair effects are $\TSCAbsSelfThreeFive\pp$ at $f\!=\!3.5$ and $\TSCAbsSelfFiveZero\pp$ at $f\!=\!5.0$, versus $\TSCAbsThreeFive\pp$ and $\TSCAbsFiveZero\pp$ for non-self pairs in the main text. The endpoint channel is therefore not merely a private memory device.

## Per-sender disaggregation reveals hidden structure

For each receiver $j$ and sender $i$, we compute the per-sender token effect within observation bins:
$$
  \delta_{i \to j}(\fhat_{\mathrm{bin}}) = \pcoopgiven{m_i\!=\!1, \fhat_{\mathrm{bin}}} - \pcoopgiven{m_i\!=\!0, \fhat_{\mathrm{bin}}}
$$ {#eq-per-sender}
and compare it to the aggregate effect, which pools across all senders:
$$
  \delta_{\text{agg}}(\fhat_{\mathrm{bin}}) = P(C \mid \exists\, k: m_k\!=\!1,\; \fhat_{\mathrm{bin}}) - P(C \mid \forall\, k: m_k\!=\!0,\; \fhat_{\mathrm{bin}})
$$ {#eq-agg}

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Aggregate versus per-sender token effects across training. Aggregate effects are near zero due to convention cancellation, while sender-conditioned effects (mean $|\delta_{i \to j}|$) remain large. Means with 95\% bootstrap CIs over 15 training seeds.}
  \tablabel{disagg}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{lccc}
    \toprule
    & \multicolumn{3}{c}{Checkpoint (episodes)} \\
    \cmidrule(lr){2-4}
    Metric & 50k & 100k & 150k \\
    \midrule
    Aggregate token effect     & $\TVaggFk\;\TVaggFkCI$ & $\TVaggHk\;\TVaggHkCI$ & $\TVaggOfk\;\TVaggOfkCI$ \\
    Per-sender token effect    & $\TVpsFk\;\TVpsFkCI$ & $\TVpsHk\;\TVpsHkCI$  & $\TVpsOfk\;\TVpsOfkCI$ \\
    Ratio (per-sender / |agg.|)  & $\TVratioFk$ & $\TVratioHk$ & $\TVratioOfk$ \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

At 50k, the aggregate token effect is only $\pvAggFkPP\pp$, while the per-sender effect is $\pvPsFkPP\pp$. The same discrepancy persists at 150k: sender-conditioned effects remain large even though the aggregate summary stays near zero.

```{python}
#| label: fig-disagg
#| fig-cap: "Aggregate vs.\\ per-sender token effects across training. The aggregate effect (signed mean $\\delta$) is near zero at all checkpoints due to convention fragmentation. The per-sender effect (mean $|\\delta_{i \\to j}|$) reveals strong sender-conditioned responses that aggregate metrics hide."
#| fig-pos: "t"
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import csv
from pathlib import Path
from collections import defaultdict

DATA_ROOT = Path("../../outputs/eval")
RECV_SEM = DATA_ROOT / "phase3_annealed_ext150k_15seeds/report/receiver_semantics_summary.csv"
RECV_PS = DATA_ROOT / "phase3_annealed_ext150k_15seeds/report/receiver_by_sender_summary.csv"

def mean(vals):
    return sum(vals) / len(vals) if vals else float("nan")

with open(RECV_SEM) as f:
    agg_rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))
agg_by_ep_seed = defaultdict(list)
for r in agg_rows:
    ep = int(float(r["checkpoint_episode"]))
    seed = int(float(r["train_seed"]))
    agg_by_ep_seed[(ep, seed)].append(float(r["delta_m1_minus_m0"]))

with open(RECV_PS) as f:
    ps_rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))
ps_by_ep_seed = defaultdict(list)
for r in ps_rows:
    if r.get("receiver_id") == r.get("sender_id") or r.get("receiver_id") == "all_agents":
        continue
    ep = int(float(r["checkpoint_episode"]))
    seed = int(float(r["train_seed"]))
    ps_by_ep_seed[(ep, seed)].append(abs(float(r["delta_m1_minus_m0"])))

eps = [50_000, 100_000, 150_000]
ep_labels = [50, 100, 150]
agg_means, ps_means = [], []
for ep in eps:
    seed_aggs = [mean(agg_by_ep_seed[(ep, s)]) for s in sorted({k[1] for k in agg_by_ep_seed if k[0] == ep})]
    seed_ps = [mean(ps_by_ep_seed[(ep, s)]) for s in sorted({k[1] for k in ps_by_ep_seed if k[0] == ep})]
    agg_means.append(mean(seed_aggs))
    ps_means.append(mean(seed_ps))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(4.4, 3.0))
bar_w = 12
x_agg = [e - bar_w/2 for e in ep_labels]
x_ps = [e + bar_w/2 for e in ep_labels]
ax.bar(x_agg, agg_means, bar_w, color="#bdd7e7", edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.5, label="Aggregate $\\delta$")
ax.bar(x_ps, ps_means, bar_w, color="#2171b5", edgecolor="black", linewidth=0.5, label="Per-sender $|\\delta_{i\\to j}|$")
ax.axhline(0, color="black", lw=0.5, ls="-")
ax.set_xlabel("Training episodes (thousands)")
ax.set_ylabel("Token effect (cooperation $\\Delta$)")
ax.set_xticks(ep_labels)
ax.legend(frameon=False, fontsize=7.5, loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, 1.18), ncol=2, columnspacing=1.0, handletextpad=0.5)
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.94])
plt.show()
```

## Polarity alignment patterns

The aggregate cancellation occurs because sender-indexed conventions often adopt opposing polarities. We summarize polarity by the sign of $P(m\!=\!1 \mid \fhat \ge 4.5) - P(m\!=\!1 \mid \fhat < 1.5)$.

Across \TVInSeeds{} seeds, alignment trajectories are heterogeneous: some seeds show stable alignment, while others flip or lose it between 50k and 150k. This confirms that convention formation in independent-learner MARL is stochastic rather than converging reliably to one shared code.

# Appendix D: Additional diagnostics on the $f\!=\!5.0$ reversal mechanism {#sec-app-f50-mech}

The main text leaves a narrower question than whether communication is ``good'' or ``bad'' in general: why does the descriptive gap reverse at $f\!=\!5.0$ by 150k even though communication remains beneficial at $f\!=\!3.5$? Two broad explanations are plausible: (i) the communication architecture may simply slow convergence toward individually rational cooperation, or (ii) training in mixed-motive regimes may induce conditional strategies that do not fully disengage once the cooperative regime is entered. The analyses in this appendix are explanatory diagnostics on the completed 15-seed checkpoints rather than a new primary identification design, but they help distinguish which interpretation is better supported.

## Checkpoint timing: early parity, late divergence

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Greedy checkpoint means at $f\!=\!5.0$ for the main communication and no-communication branches. Communication is not behind early; the gap opens only later. Mean cooperation (\%) over 15 seeds.}
  \tablabel{f50_timing}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{lcccc}
    \toprule
    Condition & 25k & 50k & 100k & 150k \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{comm\_symm}     & 62.0 & 76.1 & 55.6 & 56.1 \\
    \texttt{no\_comm\_symm} & 61.7 & 73.2 & 64.9 & 80.0 \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

This timing pattern is hard to reconcile with a pure ``communication architecture is always slower'' account. At $f\!=\!5.0$, communication is essentially tied with no-communication at 25k and ahead at 50k; the degradation appears later. The checkpoint-trend summary makes the same point from another angle: from 50k to 150k, communication drops by $20.0\pp$ on average at $f\!=\!5.0$, whereas no-communication rises by $6.8\pp$. Communication also declines over the same interval at $f\!=\!3.5$, so the issue is not late instability unique to the cooperative regime. What needs explaining is why this late drift produces a reversal at $f\!=\!5.0$ but not at $f\!=\!3.5$.

![Online training curves with greedy checkpoint anchors at $f\!=\!3.5$ and $f\!=\!5.0$. Solid curves show sampled on-policy training behavior and therefore remain descriptive; dotted markers show greedy checkpoint evaluation at 25k, 50k, 100k, and 150k. The vertical line marks the 50k continuation boundary. The key pattern is early parity and later divergence, not a uniformly worse communication branch from the start.](../../outputs/eval/paper_strengthen/iter9_online_vs_checkpoint_15seeds/online_vs_checkpoint_f35_f50.png){#fig-online-vs-checkpoint fig-pos="H"}

## Same-checkpoint controls: no positive learned-content effect at $f\!=\!5.0$

Within the fixed communication architecture, same-checkpoint continuation controls already rule out a simple positive learned-content explanation for the late $f\!=\!5.0$ behavior. At 150k, the learned branch reaches 56.1\% cooperation at $f\!=\!5.0$, compared with 54.4\% under \texttt{always\_zero}, 61.4\% under \texttt{public\_random}, and 63.1\% under \texttt{indep\_random}. The matched-stat \texttt{sender\_shuffle} continuation control does better still at 72.0\%. The contrast with $f\!=\!3.5$ is the key point: there, learned communication still stays above all of the basic controls. So the communication policy appears to retain conditional machinery in both regimes, but only the mixed regime still rewards it at 150k.

## Spell-conditioned endpoint audit: history matters more than messages

We next examined the final 150k checkpoints under greedy evaluation while conditioning on spell age since entry into the current $f\!=\!5.0$ episode segment. The goal was not to estimate a new causal effect size, but to ask what kind of intervention most changes the poor late-$f\!=\!5.0$ behavior in the communication condition: message corruption, or perturbations to temporal/history features in the wrapped observation.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
  \caption{Selected spell-conditioned diagnostics at $f\!=\!5.0$ from the final 150k checkpoints. Entries are mean cooperation (\%) over 15 seeds. ``Switch'' denotes the first round after entering an $f\!=\!5.0$ spell; ``21+'' denotes long cooperative spells.}
  \tablabel{f50_spell_audit}
  \centering
  \small
  \begin{tabular}{lccc}
    \toprule
    Variant & Overall & Switch & 21+ \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{cond1} natural & 55.5 & 72.0 & 50.7 \\
    \texttt{cond2} natural & 80.4 & 64.9 & 80.0 \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{cond1} + \texttt{zeros} & 56.0 & 67.1 & 52.4 \\
    \texttt{cond1} + \texttt{public\_random} & 57.6 & 68.9 & 53.1 \\
    \texttt{cond1} + \texttt{sender\_shuffle} & 57.4 & 72.3 & 52.0 \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{cond1} + \texttt{zero\_ewma} & 79.0 & 80.4 & 77.8 \\
    \texttt{cond1} + \texttt{clamp\_temporal\_low} & 80.0 & 80.8 & 78.7 \\
    \texttt{cond1} + \texttt{clamp\_temporal\_high} & 23.6 & 24.2 & 23.2 \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

Three features of Table \ref{tab:f50_spell_audit} are especially informative.

First, the late-$f\!=\!5.0$ decay is real and asymmetric across conditions. In the communication condition, cooperation starts relatively high immediately after a regime switch (72.0\%) but falls steadily across longer $f\!=\!5.0$ spells, reaching 50.7\% in the 21+ bucket. The no-communication condition shows the opposite qualitative pattern: it starts lower right after the switch (64.9\%) and remains high in long cooperative spells (80.0\% overall; 80.0\% in the 21+ bucket). This is the pattern expected under a late conditionality or overwrite account, not under a pure always-slower architecture account.

Second, corrupting the online message stream leaves the decay largely intact. Replacing natural messages with zeros, public random bits, or sender-shuffled bits changes the communication-condition mean only modestly (roughly 56--58\% overall, versus 55.5\% natural), and the long-spell bucket remains near 52--53\%. So the poor 150k performance at $f\!=\!5.0$ is not primarily an endpoint dependence on the exact online message content.

Third, intervening on temporal/history features changes behavior much more strongly. Zeroing the EWMA cooperation signal or clamping the temporal-history proxy low raises $f\!=\!5.0$ cooperation to about 79--80\%, close to the no-communication level, while clamping that proxy high collapses cooperation to about 24\%. This indicates that the problematic late policy is strongly history-conditioned: by 150k, the communication-trained agents appear to be using temporal or lagged social features in a way that is maladaptive within long cooperative spells.

## Interpretation and scope

Taken together, these diagnostics support explanation (ii) more strongly than explanation (i). The communication branch is not handicapped from the start; it is competitive or better early at $f\!=\!5.0$ and only falls behind later. Same-checkpoint controls then show why this late drift matters differently across regimes: at $f\!=\!3.5$, learned communication still outperforms the basic controls, so conditional responses retain strategic value, whereas at $f\!=\!5.0$ the same learned channel offers no corresponding advantage. Finally, the poor late-$f\!=\!5.0$ state is much more sensitive to temporal/history features than to message corruption. The most coherent reading is therefore that mixed-regime conditionality remains encoded in the communication policy and fails to disengage in long cooperative spells.

We do not claim that architecture cost is absent. These diagnostics do not retrain an exactly capacity-matched no-message architecture under a continuation protocol, so a contribution from extra inputs or optimization complexity cannot be ruled out completely. But the current evidence is more consistent with a late mixed-regime conditioning or overwrite story than with a purely static architecture-complexity explanation.

```{=latex}
\newpage
\section*{NeurIPS Paper Checklist}

\begin{enumerate}

\item {\bf Claims}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: The abstract and introduction clearly state four contributions supported by the experimental results in the main body.

\item {\bf Limitations}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: The \emph{Discussion} section discusses limitations including single game, minimal vocabulary, PPO coupling, and seed heterogeneity.

\item {\bf Theory assumptions and proofs}
    \item[] Answer: \answerNA{}
    \item[] Justification: This is an empirical paper; no theoretical results are presented.

\item {\bf Experimental result reproducibility}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: Appendix A provides complete hyperparameters. Code will be released upon acceptance.

\item {\bf Open access to data and code}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: Anonymized code and evaluation scripts will be provided as supplementary material. Full code release upon acceptance.

\item {\bf Experimental setting/details}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: The \emph{Setup} section and Appendix A provide all training and evaluation details.

\item {\bf Experiment statistical significance}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: We report mean $\pm$ SEM across 15 seeds, bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples) for effect sizes, and exact paired sign-flip tests for primary paired comparisons.

\item {\bf Experiments compute resources}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: Experiments ran on an Apple M1 MacBook Pro (10 cores, 16GB RAM) and an IWR cluster node (quadxeon7). Each 150k-episode training run takes approximately 9 hours. Total compute for all experiments (including pilots, ablations, and the 15-seed expansion) is approximately 1500 CPU-hours.

\item {\bf Code of ethics}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: This research studies cooperation in abstract game-theoretic settings with no human subjects, sensitive data, or dual-use concerns.

\item {\bf Broader impacts}
    \item[] Answer: \answerNA{}
    \item[] Justification: This is basic research on emergent communication in abstract multi-agent systems. We do not foresee direct negative societal impacts.

\item {\bf Safeguards}
    \item[] Answer: \answerNA{}
    \item[] Justification: No models or datasets with misuse risk are released.

\item {\bf Licenses for existing assets}
    \item[] Answer: \answerNA{}
    \item[] Justification: No pre-existing datasets or models are used. The codebase builds on standard PyTorch.

\item {\bf New assets}
    \item[] Answer: \answerYes{}
    \item[] Justification: We will release the complete codebase (training, evaluation, analysis) and trained checkpoints upon acceptance.

\item {\bf Crowdsourcing and research with human subjects}
    \item[] Answer: \answerNA{}
    \item[] Justification: No human subjects involved.

\item {\bf Institutional review board (IRB) approvals or equivalent for research with human subjects}
    \item[] Answer: \answerNA{}
    \item[] Justification: No human subjects involved.

\item {\bf Declaration of LLM usage}
    \item[] Answer: \answerNA{}
    \item[] Justification: No LLMs are used as part of the core research methodology. LLMs were used only for writing assistance.

\end{enumerate}
```
